coredata.dc:title: Upcycling drinking bottle waste to intercalated 2D-0D carbon architectures and its supercapacitor applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Yixian
Liao, Yixin
Zhang, Jinge
Zhong, Haixu
Yang, Yu
Wen, Xiufang
Du, Weimin
Wang, Shengnian
coredata.dc:description: Despite its many benefits to our modern life, plastic contributes a large volume of solid waste that increasingly threatens our environment and health. Through a hydrothermal depolymerization and carbonization process that involves nitric acid and ethanol, drinking bottles that are made of polyethylene terephthalate are successfully converted into carbon quantum dots (CDs) and thin carbon nanosheets simultaneously with the former intercalated between layers of the latter to form a unique ball-sheet architecture. When used as electrode material, the stacking carbon nanosheets in this plastic-derived, ball-sheet carbon (PBSC) structure create a conductive network to allow rapid transport of ions and electrons while the well-dispersed CDs offer a large surface area and numerous sites to host them. The supercapacitors made of these PBSC electrodes exhibit double-layer capacitor behaviors with a specific capacity reaching 237 F/g at a charge rate of 1 A/g and excellent cycling stability. Our efforts offer a new route to upcycle plastic waste as valuable energy storage materials, which may help boost its recycling and the sustainable deployment of various clean energy resources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plastic upcycling
Carbon dots
Supercapacitors
Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)
Plastics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discussions on synthesis mechanism and optimization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation [OIA 2217824, 2418390], the Louisiana Board of Regents, ITRS [LEQSF(2023-26)-RD-B-02], and Louisiana Harrelson Family Professorship.

This work was supported by the National Science Foundation [OIA 2217824, 2418390], the Louisiana Board of Regents, ITRS [LEQSF(2023-26)-RD-B-02], and Louisiana Harrelson Family Professorship.The following is the Supplementary data to this article.> [Fugure presented

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a), AFM (b), TEM (c), and HR TEM (d) images of PET PBSCC2H5OH. The inset of panel b shows the height profile on the green line of the AFM image (the sharp peak over 4 nm is contributed to a CD aggregate, the white spot on the line while the rest peaks are for individual CDs). The inset of panel c shows the histogram of particle size measurement of the imaged CDs, and the inset of panel d is a high-magnitude image of PET PBSCC2H5OH. The scale bar in the inset of panel d presents 5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Spectrum Characterization of PBSCs derived from PET involving ethanol (PBSCC2H5OH) and ethylene glycol (PBSCHOC2H4OH): XRD spectrum (a); XPS survey scan (b) and a detailed scan of N 1s (c); FTIR (d).

The electrochemical performance of electrodes made of the activated PBSCs in 6M KOH solution. (a) CV scanning curves of PBSCs at various rates; GCD testing of PBSCs synthesized with ethanol and ethylene glycol (b) at 1A/g and (c) at different current densities, (d) for PBSCs synthesized at different temperatures (at a current density of 1 A/g), (e) with pure PET and PET drink bottle waste (at a current density of 2 A/g). (f) Cycling stability test at a current density of 2 A/g.

(a) Nyquist plots and (b) the corresponding fitting on an equivalent circuit of electrodes made of PBSCC2H5OH: fresh and after 12000 cycles; (c) Nyquist plots of electrodes made of fresh PBSCC2H5OH and PBSCHOC2H4OH. The corresponding fitting on an equivalent circuit of PBSCHOC2H4OH is also given in panel b. The impedance spectrum was done with a voltage amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range of 0.01Hz–100 kHz.

DFT calculation results. (a) molecular structure, HOMO LUMO, and energy gap) on different CD models (pyridinic-x and pyrrolic-x, with x = 1 pyridine or pyrrole only; x = 2 having an additional –NH2 group; x = 3 having an additional COOH group and x = 4 having an additional OH group). Electrostatic potential (b) and the absorption (c) of 3 K+ of different CD models.

Schematic of the synthesis of plastic-derived, ball-sheet structure carbon (PBSC) from PET involving nitric acid and ethanol. Plastic waste (PET) is depolymerized in hydrothermal conditions involving nitric acid and ethanol (step 1); partially degraded fragments are cross-linked into carbon nanosheet (step 2a); deeply degraded oligomers/monomers are condensed into carbon quantum dots (step 2b); CDs are further anchored onto carbon nanosheet as CD/nanosheet structures (step 3), and multiple CD/nanosheets are packing into ball-sheet structures (PBSCs) through available surface groups on CDs and carbon nanosheets (step 4).
Schematic of the synthesis of plastic-derived, ball-sheet structure carbon (PBSC) from PET involving nitric acid and ethanol.

head.ce:title: Upcycling drinking bottle waste to intercalated 2D-0D carbon architectures and its supercapacitor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering, Institute for Micromanufacturing, Louisiana Tech University, PO Box 10137, Ruston, LA, 71272, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Engineering, Institute for Micromanufacturing, Louisiana Tech University, PO Box 10137, Ruston, LA, 71272, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anyang Normal University, Anyang, Henan, 455002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anyang Normal University, Anyang, Henan, 455002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite its many benefits to our modern life, plastic contributes a large volume of solid waste that increasingly threatens our environment and health. Through a hydrothermal depolymerization and carbonization process that involves nitric acid and ethanol, drinking bottles that are made of polyethylene terephthalate are successfully converted into carbon quantum dots (CDs) and thin carbon nanosheets simultaneously with the former intercalated between layers of the latter to form a unique ball-sheet architecture. When used as electrode material, the stacking carbon nanosheets in this plastic-derived, ball-sheet carbon (PBSC) structure create a conductive network to allow rapid transport of ions and electrons while the well-dispersed CDs offer a large surface area and numerous sites to host them. The supercapacitors made of these PBSC electrodes exhibit double-layer capacitor behaviors with a specific capacity reaching 237 F/g at a charge rate of 1 A/g and excellent cycling stability. Our efforts offer a new route to upcycle plastic waste as valuable energy storage materials, which may help boost its recycling and the sustainable deployment of various clean energy resources.

We upcycled drinking bottles into supercapacitor electrode materials. With carbon quantum dots intercalated between layers of carbon nanosheets, this ball-sheet carbon architecture offers numerous sites to host ions and electrons and space and a conductive network for their rapid transport. Double-layer capacitor behaviors are exhibited with a specific capacity of 237 F/g at 1 A/g and excellent cycling stability.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Our heavy dependence on fossil fuels and their excessive consumption causes many global challenges regarding environmental pollution and climate change. It urges the mission to search for alternative clean energy resources like wind, ocean, and solar power on a much larger scale in the near future [1]. Efficient, effective, and economical storage of the generated clean energy is crucial for their sustainable deployment as many of these renewable resources have intermittent characteristics [2]. Supercapacitor is one of such attractive energy storage systems because of its fast charge/discharge rates, high power density, and long cycle life [3–6]. Studies have shown that the electrode material itself and its architectural features such as surface area, conductivity, and active sites for ion hosting are critical for supercapacitors to achieve superior electrochemical performance [7–9]. Among different electrode materials of supercapacitors, various formats of carbon dominate because of their excellent conductivity, rich active sites, and good chemical stability [10–12]. Three-dimensional porous carbon material (e.g., activated carbon) that is prepared from either physical or chemical activation of various types of carbonaceous sources including wood, coal, and biomass (e.g., plant and animal debris) carries a huge specific surface area and numerous hosting sites, but unfortunately only moderate conductivity [13,14]. One-dimensional (e.g., carbon fibers) or two-dimensional (e.g., graphene) carbon structures generally have a connected network with excellent electrical conductivity, while relatively limited surface area and high cost (or low manufacturing capacity) [15–17]. Zero-dimensional carbon such as carbon nanoparticles can provide flexible specific surface area attributed to their tunable size, size distribution, and shape [18,19]. However, their discrete nature requires the assistance of conductive, non-active additives or other carbon structures to make a working electrode. Combining different types of carbon materials seems attractive to preserve the advantages of each component to achieve superior storage capacity, high conductivity, and cycling stability. Many attempts have been made with this strategy [20,21], which usually involves multiple steps to prepare the needed composite electrodes and/or sophisticated carbon structures. For example, to form an integrated zero-dimensional (0D) and two-dimensional (2D) carbon electrode in which 0D carbon particles are intercalated in the gaps of 2D carbon sheets, each carbon structure is synthesized separately at first, followed by delaminating the 2D carbon sheets mechanically (e.g., sonication) and/or chemically (e.g., adding surfactant), dispersing the 0D carbon on their surface, and re-stacking them into an intercalated architecture. The assembly often faces great challenges during actual manufacturing, namely the aggregation of 0D and/or 2D carbon materials because of their large surface energy and strong interactions. In many cases, it leads to heterogeneous morphologies across the finished electrodes, and as a consequence, large variations of the later assembled supercapacitors on their electrochemical performance.

Herein we investigate a one-step synthesis strategy to create a unique ball (0D)-sheet (2D) carbon structure with a connected conductive network and numerous ion hosting sites to serve as ideal supercapacitor electrodes. Specifically, carbon dots (CDs) and thin carbon nanosheets are simultaneously produced with the former intercalated between different layers of the latter. To further guarantee the sustainable nature of our technology, plastic waste such as discarded drinking bottles that are made of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is adopted as the carbon source during the hydrothermal synthesis of this plastic-derived, ball-sheet carbon structure (designated as “PBSC” in later discussions). Within one step, we successfully produced thin 2D carbon nanosheets on which CDs of 1–2 nm are grafted and well dispersed on their surface (Scheme 1 ). After drying and activation, the received carbon colloids with this PBSC architecture are coated on the surface of a nickel foam substrate for electrochemical testing. Their supercapacitor performance is investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. In this supercapacitor material, CDs provide a large surface area and numerous active sites to host ions for their rich surface defects/ligands. The 2D carbon nanosheets help anchor CDs to avoid large aggregations and build up a connected, conductive carbon network needed for a working electrode of supercapacitors. As the formation of CDs (0D carbon) and carbon nanosheets (2D carbon) occurs simultaneously in the same synthesis solution, the surface energy of individual carbon objects (both CDs and carbon sheets) is greatly suppressed by their surrounding environment so that CDs are naturally confined between the new formed 2D carbon nanosheets while retaining their good dispersion status after drying. In this way, the common challenges of dispersion and intercalation of 0D carbon in a 2D structure are wisely avoided. Besides the advantages of easy manufacturing and effective architecture for electrical energy storage, our method uses plastic waste and upcycles them into clean energy materials, which may also benefit the global mission on energy and environmental sustainability. Unlike another sustainable organic carbon resources, biomass waste which often has a porous structure and gets it inherited to later formed carbon materials [22,23], plastic-derived counterparts usually require additional engineering efforts like what are presented in this work to create such preferred features for energy storage applications. However, plastics provide relatively higher content of fixed carbon and lower impurities as the raw material than biomass for porous carbons. In addition, plastic waste has a larger annual production capacity to allow sustainable supply to industrial-level applications when compared to the seasonal availability of biomass waste. Nonetheless, our new hydrothermal transformation of PET exhibits higher carbon retention and richer surface functionality than traditional pyrolysis-based treatments [24,25]. The novel porous structure of this PBSC carbon may find other promising adsorption-based applications in the capture of important liquid/gas products or contaminants. As the fourth-most-produced polymer (accounting for about 8 % of all plastic products or >30 million tons of production worldwide [26]), we expect our efforts to create a new utilization revenue of this plastic waste to help promote the collection and recycling of plastic waste with economic intensive and move forwards to the closed-loop life cycle of plastics.

Pure polyethylene terephthalate (PET) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Samples of commercial PET were cut from drinking water bottles. Nitric acid (70 %), ethanol, and ethylene glycol were purchased from Fisher Scientific. All chemicals and reagents were used as received without further purification unless specified.

A washed drinking water bottle was cut into pieces. Each PET sample of 0.5 g was loaded in a 50-mL PPL-lined autoclave, in which 10 mL of DI water, 2 mL of nitric acid (71 %), and 2 mL ethanol (99 %) were added. The hydrothermal synthesis was carried out at 200 °C for 8 h. The received black solution was first washed using DI water, followed by centrifugation at 3500 rpm multiple times until pH ≥ 6. The solid sample (∼0.4–0.45 g) was collected after drying in a vacuum oven overnight at 70 °C and designated as PBSCC2H5OH (PET Ball-Sheet Carbon with C2H5OH). As for comparison purposes, carbon samples were also made by replacing ethanol with ethylene glycol during the hydrothermal synthesis, which are designated as PBSCHOC2H4OH. Pure PET was sometimes used for comparison and these PBSC samples were specified when mentioned. The received samples were kept in an ultra-dry glove box (with water content <0.1 ppm) for a week to eliminate the surface adsorbed water.

The size and structure of PBSCs were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 1400, JEOL), and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Hitachi H-9500, Hitachi). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of dried PBSCs were recorded to identify the functional groups on the surface (Thermo Scientific SMART FT-IR spectrometer). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Scienta Omicron XPS) analysis was done to reveal the chemical states of elements in the as-synthesized PBSCs with the following operation conditions: 10 sweeps for the survey scans and 200 sweeps for each detailed scan of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s. The pass energy was set to 40 eV with a step size of 0.2 eV and a dwell time of 200 ms for all samples.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was done on a Bruker D8 diffractometer, using Cu-Kα radiation at room temperature and instrumental settings of 40 kV and 40 mA. Data were recorded in the 2θ range of 6–60° with an angular step size of 0.02°. Nitrogen adsorption isotherms were obtained at −196 °C on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer with a relative pressure range from 0.05 to 0.99. Before measurement, samples were degassed at 300 °C for 6 h. Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to quantify unreacted plastics and solid products using a TGA 8000 Thermogravimetric Analyzer (PerkinElmer Inc). The weight loss of solid samples was monitored through thermal scanning from 30 to 600 °C at a ramp rate of 10 °C/min in a nitrogen atmosphere.

The PBSCPET/C2H5OH samples were first mixed with 6M KOH (mass ratio: 1:6) and calcined in nitrogen gas at 550 °C for 2 h for surface activation. The activated PBSCC2H5OH powders were then washed using DI water and centrifugation until pH ∼7. After discarding the supernatant, samples were dried under vacuum at 70 °C overnight.

The received solid (∼0.5g) was mixed with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and super P carbon black at a mass ratio of 80 ∶ 10 ∶ 10 in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP). The paste was then blade-coated onto a foamed nickel sheet with an area of ∼1 cm2 (this gives a load mass of ∼5 mg). After drying at 80 °C for 12 h, the prepared electrode was placed in an electrochemical cell that was filled with a solution of 6M KOH electrolytes. A three-electrode system was adopted with platinum rod and calomel electrodes as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively.

CHI 760E electrochemical workstation was used for most electrochemical tests with the voltage window set between −1.1-0 V. After galvanostatic charge/discharge test, the specific capacitance was calculated according to equation (1)
(1)
C g
=
I Δ t
m Δ V
where C
g is the specific capacitance of electrode mass (F/g); I is the current (A); Δ t is the discharge time (s); ΔV is the voltage (V); m is the mass of the active material in the working electrode (g).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted on CHI 760E. The AC perturbation signal was set at 5 mV with a frequency range of 10−1 -105 Hz. The Zview2 software was used to extract the parameters of electronic units in a pre-set equivalent electronic circuit of the tested batteries.

Calculations were performed by first-principles density functional theory (DFT) via Gaussian 09. The simulation was optimized using the Becke three-parameter hybrid functional with the Lee-Yang-Perdew (B3LYP) correlation functional and the highly accurate 6-31G** basis set [27,28]. All studied structures undergo complete relaxation with no imaginary frequency indicating the complete optimization of the structure. GaussView 5.0 was used for different molecular visualization. The calculation formula for adsorption energy is as follows [29]:
E ads
=
E
CQD + n
K +
−
E CQD
− n
E
K +
where E CQD+n K+ represents the total energy of the absorption system when the K+ ions are absorbed on the surface of CQDs. E CQD and E K+ are the energy of the CQDs and K+ ions after structure optimization, respectively.

The morphology of the received PBSCs was characterized by SEM, AFM, and TEM. The SEM image shows a flake-like morphology of the received carbon samples after the hydrothermal treatment of PET drinking bottle waste (Fig. 1 a). On the surface of individual carbon flakes, many tiny dots can be clearly seen. The AFM image further reveals that they are “islands” of 500–2000 nm (Fig. 1b) made of many individual CDs as confirmed by TEM characterization (Fig. 1c & d). The CDs are well dispersed in these individual clusters (i.e., “island”) on carbon flakes, having an average size of 2.6 nm (inset of Fig. 2 c) and a topographic height of ∼1–2 nm (inset of Fig. 1b). Some large particles of 20–40 nm can also be found scattered on the surface of thin carbon flakes (i.e., the white dots in the AFM image of Fig. 1b), which are believed to be aggregates of individual CDs. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image further confirms that clear crystalline lattice fringes spread over the entire particle of the received CDs (Fig. 1d). The lattice spacing is found ∼0.21 nm, attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene, suggesting that those particles are individual CDs, not PET debris of incomplete transformation.

To further confirm these tiny dots are indeed CDs, XRD and fluorescence spectra of the received samples were also measured. The XRD spectrum of activated PBSCC2H5OH (Fig. 2a) exhibits a broad peak between 20 and 30° for 2θ, corresponding to the (002) plane, and a visible peak near 42.3° for the (100) plane of aromatic layers. Although signals of these peaks are quite noisy due to the limited reflection area of those small carbon crystallites, the stacking structure of aromatic layers is confirmed, endorsing what was found in the HR-TEM images. No obvious impurities (e.g., metal or metal oxide) are found in the XRD (or the other spectral results of this PBSC C2H5OH) which rules out potential interference of impurities in the collected samples (e.g., ∼10 % inorganic additives in commercial PET products) during later supercapacity tests. As for photoluminescence measurement, a strong emission peak centered near 430 nm is clearly shown under a UV excitation light of 320 nm (Supp Fig. S1). A slight red shift of the peak center is found in the emission spectra when the excitation wavelength varies from 320 nm to 420 nm. The strongest fluorescence emission centered at 450 nm is received under an excited light of 340 nm, with a quantum yield calculated to be 10.2 %. These results demonstrate that those tiny dots found in microscopic images are CDs.

Given the importance of the surface area and porous texture to supercapacitor applications, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the activated PBSCC2H5OH were measured (Supp Fig. S2). Three molecule adsorption stages are found, a steep one at P/P0 < 0.02, followed by a slowly climbing one before P/P0 = 0.40, and another gradually growing one up to high P/P0. The first stage is for the filling of available micropores while the second one suggests the existence of mesoporous space that is created by the PBSC structure. The third curvature is attributed to unrestricted adsorption in many carbon materials. A hysteresis loop at 0.40 < P/P0 < 0.95 shows mixed features of H3/H4 loops which are often found with aggregates of plate-like particles and micro-mesoporous carbons. This further confirms the existence of a hierarchically porous structure of the received PBSCC2H5OH. The specific surface area of PBSCC2H5OH is calculated to be 150 m2g-1 according to the BET method, together with a total pore volume of 0.209 cm3/g, and a mean BJH pore diameter of 12 nm. Although the BET surface area of the received PBSCC2H5OH carbon is not very attractive when compared to many activated carbon materials [30,31], the large pore volume and average pore size contributed by its unique ball-sheet structure and mesoporosity promote ion transport to the active sites of the electrode surface for better access during the charge/discharge cycles, pivotal for supercapacitor applications.

XPS spectra display that carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen are the three major elements of the PBSCs derived from PET waste (Fig. 2b). Similar atomic compositions are found in both samples that have the second precursor as ethanol (C2H5OH) and ethylene glycol (HOC2H4OH), respectively. The PBSC synthesized with ethanol and PET (designated as PBSCC2H5OH) has a slightly higher nitrogen dopant than the one transformed from PET and ethylene glycol (designated as PBSCHOC2H4OH): 6.1 at% for PBSCC2H5OH versus 4.3 at% for PBSCHOC2H4OH. To further reveal the chemical states of these three elements, detailed XPS scans of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s were done. Three major deconvoluted peaks (located at ∼284.3 eV, ∼285.0 eV, and ∼288.1 eV) are found in the C 1s spectra of both PBSC samples (Supp Fig. S3a), which are associated with C=C/C–C, C–N/C–O, and C=O groups, respectively. Unlike their similar portion of C=O bonds, fewer C=C/C–C bonds or benzene ring structures are found in the PBSCC2H5OH sample (Supp Fig. 3a and Supp Table 1). A larger deconvoluted peak at 285.0 eV is found in the C 1s peaks of the PBSCC2H5OH sample. Considering a similar level of C=O groups (or possibly retained ester groups from PET) in both samples, this suggests that the larger portion of C 1s peak at 285.0 eV in PBSCC2H5OH is likely a mixed contribution of more C–N and C–OH bonds. Although an extra 4.6 % portion of the C–O bonds is also found in this PBSCC2H5OH sample according to its O 1s peaks (532.6 eV), it cannot account for all the 8.2 % more C–N/C–O bonds portion in PBSCC2H5OH than that made of PBSCHOC2H4OH according to the C 1s peak at 285.0 eV (Supp Fig. S3b and Supp Table 1). The difference (i.e., 3.6 %) must be attributed to the higher nitrogen doping and C–N bonds in the framework of PBSCC2H5OH and/or the functional groups on the surface. To further find out the format of nitrogen in those PBSCs, high-resolution spectra of N 1s (Fig. 2c) were collected. Three deconvoluted peaks were identified at 398.8 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.5 eV in these PBSCs, which can be assigned to pyridinic, pyridone, and pyrrolic nitrogen structures, respectively. No oxidized nitrogen bonds such as oxidized pyridine nitrogen (representative peak near 402–405 eV) or –NO2 groups (near 405 eV) are found in the PBSCC2H5OH, while a small portion is indeed present in the PBSCHOC2H4OH. More importantly, pyrrolic nitrogen bonds dominate among various C–N bonds in PBSCC2H5OH, while such dominance shifts to pyridine nitrogen bonds in the PBSCHOC2H4OH sample (Supp Table 1). These findings also match the fact of more C=C/C–C bonds in PBSCHOC2H4OH while a larger portion of C–N/C–O bonds in PBSCC2H5OH according to their C 1s peaks (Supp Table 1). Nonetheless, the XPS results indicate that nitrogen-doped PBSCs were successfully synthesized from PET waste. Although the surface of both PBSCs (PBSCC2H5OH and PBSCHOC2H4OH) is functionalized with C–OH, COOH, C–NH, and O=C–NH < groups, more pyrrolic structures are found in PBSCC2H5OH while more pyridine nitrogen bonds in PBSCHOC2H4OH.

FTIR was further used to examine the function groups on these activated PBSCs (Fig. 2d). The spectra of PBSCC2H5OH derived from both pure PET and PET drinking bottle waste (with additives) exhibit similar patterns (Supp Fig. S4). Typical broad stretching vibration absorption peaks of –OH are shown between 3000 and 3750 cm−1 and antisymmetric vibration of –CH3 near 2950 cm−1, suggesting the existence of a large number of these groups. Correspondingly, there is a strong absorption peak for the in-plane bending vibration of phenolic OH at ∼1386 cm−1. It indicates that the –OH groups are present in both phenolic and carboxyl structures in those PBSC samples. The peaks between 1075 and 1120 cm−1 are contributed by the stretching vibration of C=O lactone, suggesting that some ester bonds may be retained in these PBSCs after the hydrothermal reactions. Another C=O stretching vibration peak near 1717 cm−1 is due to the π-π interactions of benzene rings and substituted pyrrole structures. The peak near 1650 cm−1 is caused by the C=C stretching vibration from the disubstituted benzene rings. The relative area difference of these two peaks supports our claims that the formation of more new ring structures in PBSCC2H5OH while the better reservation of original benzene rings in PBSCHOC2H4OH. Small peaks located between 700–1000 cm−1 are attributed to out-of-plane CH bending vibrations on the carbon skeleton of these PBSCs, particularly an obvious one located near 740 cm−1 for the vibration of CH from benzene rings. As for nitrogen bonds in the received PBSCs, the C–N stretching vibration at 1277 cm−1 is very clear, and so is a broad peak centered at 3400 cm−1 that presents –NH groups in those PBSCs. When compared to PBSCHOC2H4OH, the broad FTIR peak of the –NH group at 3400 cm−1 in PBSCC2H5OH is less obvious while the C–N stretching vibration at 1277 cm−1 is much stronger. As for the functional groups associated with benzene rings, some are stronger in PBSCC2H5OH (e.g., pyrrolic bonds at 1717 cm−1 and Ar-CH at 737 cm−1), some are weaker (e.g., C=C at 1648 cm−1), and others are similar (e.g., Ar-OH at 1386 cm−1). It seems that more new rings are formed during the synthesis of both types of PBSC while the type of rings and functional groups in these PBSCs are different when a diol (i.e., ethylene glycol) or primary alcohol (i.e., ethanol) is involved during the transformation of PET.

The electrochemical capacitance of these activated PBSCs was evaluated in a three-electrode setup with a Pt rod and calomel electrode as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The tests were done in a 6M KOH electrolyte solution at room temperature with a voltage window of −1.1-0 V. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning curves of the as-prepared PBSC electrodes present typical quasi-rectangular traces without obvious redox peaks in a potential range of −1.1-0 V (vs. Hg/HgO). This confirms that the capacitor works primarily on the electrical double-layer mechanism like many other carbon electrodes. The curve shape is well preserved at various scanning rates, reflecting the excellent rate stability of this PBSC electrode. The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) results of these PBSC electrodes exhibit some derivations from the ideal triangular shape but have very little iR drop (Fig. 3b). This indicates their good conductivity and capacitance behavior. At a charge rate of 1 A/g, the PBSCC2H5OH electrodes show a much larger specific capacitance than that made of PBSCHOC2H4OH (239 F/g for PBSCC2H5OH and 150 F/g for PBSCHOC2H4OH). When raising the scan rate, a capacitance drop was observed for both types of PBSC electrodes just like most other supercapacitor electrodes. Under a current density of 2 A/g, the specific capacity of PBSCC2H5OH electrode descends to 198 F/g, but still much higher than that of PBSCHOC2H4OH (99 F/g), as shown in Supp Fig. S5. Examined in a broader charge rate range (from 1 A/g- 50 A/g), the capacity of the PBSCC2H5OH electrode, as shown in Fig. 3c, decreases slowly to 198 F/g (2A/g), 168 F/g (5 A/g), 147 F/g (10 A/g), 129 F/g (20 A/g), and 96 F/g (50 A/g), respectively (Note: the corresponding current density is given in the brackets). PBSCC2H5OH synthesized at different hydrothermal temperatures were further tested to decide the optimal synthesis conditions for the best electrochemical performance. As shown in Fig. 3d, the PBSCC2H5OH synthesized at 200 °C shows the largest specific capacitance, though the derivations among PBSCs synthesized at three different temperatures are not big. As plastic waste usually carries 10 % or less of additives other than the polymer itself in their manufacturing recipe, we further checked the performance of the PBSCC2H5OH electrodes made from both pure PET and PET drinking water bottle waste. The GCD curves of PBSCC2H5OH from different purities display similar shapes and specific capacitance (Fig. 3e). This suggests that those additives were removed from the received solid carbon PBSCs during the washing steps, and an extra purification step is not necessary for the PBSCs derived from real PET waste. They could be directly used to make supercapacitors with reliable electrochemical performance. To further evaluate the possibility of its practical use in supercapacitors, the long-term cycling stability of this PBSCC2H5OH electrode made of plastic waste was further tested. As shown in Fig. 3f, after 12,000 cycles, approximately 98 % of its initial specific capacitance is retained at the charge rate of 2 A/g, and a similar shape of the GCD curves is received when compared to the fresh electrode.

To better understand the electrochemical behavior of these PBSC electrodes at the electrode/electrolyte interface, EIS analysis was done. Nyquist plots were made to extract information on charge transfer, heterogeneity at the electrode interfaces, the derivation from an ideal capacitor, and ion diffusion. As shown in Fig. 4 a, a typical curve with a semicircle followed by a straight line is shown in the Nyquist diagrams of both a fresh PBSC electrode and the one after 12000 cycles. Their EIS patterns are almost identical, confirming our early observation of their superior long-term stability. Using Zview software, an equivalent circuit model that includes one solution resistor (R1), one charge transfer resistor (R2), one non-ideal constant phase element (CPE), and one Warburg element (W) is applied to fit the EIS data in each case (Fig. 4b). The small value of R1 in both cases (i.e., fresh and cycled electrodes) indicates the low resistance in electrolytes and at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The rapid charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface is also confirmed by the small value of R2. A total resistance of 2.544 Ω % for R1 + R2 reveals the excellent electrical conductivity of the fresh PBSCC2H5OH electrode. The slight deviation from 1.0 on the “P” value of the CPE unit suggests how close these PBSCC2H5OH electrodes are to an ideal double-layer capacitor. The presence of CDs between the 2D carbon nanosheets, though significantly increases the total number of the host sites for ions, does not add much surface heterogeneity or tortuosity of the migration pathways of ions that is often seen in common porous materials. The value of Warburg impedance (W) units also reflects that effective ion diffusion occurs on these PBSC electrodes. Nevertheless, the little variation in the values of these elements after cycling suggests that the migration situation on ions and electrons did not change much after the long-term operation, which is consistent with our stability testing results of these electrodes for excellent capacitance retention. As a comparison, the EIS pattern in the Nyquist plot of the PBSCHOC2H4OH electrode shifts significantly (Fig. 4c). Both larger solution resistance and charge transfer resistance are found for the PBSCHOC2H4OH electrode (4.233 Ω for R1 + R2) than that made of PBSCC2H5OH, as shown in Fig. 4b. It also derives more from an ideal, reversible capacitor based on the fitting parameters of its other equivalent circuit model elements. Nevertheless, these results confirm that the PBSCC2H5OH electrodes have excellent electrical conductivity and consequently superior electrochemical kinetics performance.

To further investigate what may cause the different performances in electrochemical testing of these two types of PBSCs (that are derived from PET and ethanol or ethylene glycol), DFT calculation was also done to reveal the electronic properties of a CD that has pyridinic or pyrrolic nitrogen presented and their interactions with potassium ions. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the band gap of a pyrrolic nitrogen structure on a CD is always smaller than its pyridinic counterpart no matter whether or what other functional groups are also on the carbon structure. In the presence of KOH, unlike electron-withdrawing pyridine groups, pyrrolic CDs with higher HOMO and LUMO exhibit electron-donating groups which assure their better binding ability with K+ ions. The electrostatic potential calculation given in Fig. 5b also suggests that pyrrolic nitrogen can activate CDs better than pyridinic nitrogen, thereby providing more active sites for ion adsorption. Further calculations on the adsorption energy of K+ ions on these CD structures further find that the adsorption energy of K+ ions (3 ions/simulation domain) is always lower on various pyrrolic structures than their pyridine counterparts (Fig. 5c) due to the strong electronegativity of the nitrogen atom of pyridine. The low adsorption energy and more host sites contributed by the dominated pyrrolic structures in PBSCC2H5OH seem to account for its much higher electric capacitance, which agrees with some pioneer observations on CDs [32–34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussions on synthesis mechanism and optimization
ce:section.ce:para: During the synthesis of this ball-sheet intercalated carbon structure, the formation of CDs and carbon nanosheets is believed to go through different routes. In the presence of ethanol and nitric acid, some ester bonds on the polymer chain of PET must have broken first to lightly degrade the polymer into short-chain fragments of various sizes (Fig. 6 , step 1). These degraded fragments are either further depolymerized deeply into oligomers or monomers (i.e., phthalic acid (TPA) and ethylene glycol (EG)) or re-linked together (Fig. 6, step 2). The degraded mixture then experiences carbonization, among which PET fragments are cross-linked into nanosheets (step 2a) while monomers or oligomers are condensed into CDs (step 2b). Condensation of these carbonaceous molecules into stacked carbon-ring structure follows a mixture of two different routes: one is the coking formation mechanism that takes place in an acid environment while the other is often suggested in CD production in which the carboxyl groups and amine groups form different molecules through C–N bonds. Through similar condensation mechanisms, the newly formed CDs could also be anchored on those carbon nanosheets (step 3), and further stacked up into ball-sheet structures when the CD anchoring happens among multiple carbon nanosheets (step 4). With only one hydroxyl group on ethanol, more defects or five-atom ring structures will be created in the formed carbon structure with a porous honeycomb structure. With rich hydroxyl, carboxyl, and various nitrogen-containing functional groups (pyridinic, pyrrolic, and pyridonic nitrogen) left on the surface, they provide numerous hosting sites for ions during the charge step of a supercapacitor. The unique ball-sheet intercalated carbon structure offers easy access to these sites and fast diffusion pathways for ions that are necessary for the charge/discharge processes of supercapacitors. The participation of ethanol exaggerates these effects more than ethylene glycol, which explains the electrochemical performance difference of samples synthesized by these two different second precursors.

Acid is known to facilitate the degradation of PET via so-called acidolysis [35]. However, without the involvement of ethanol, acidolysis alone (i.e., PET in nitric acid) will receive only a clear yellow solution without any solid product, and the received solution emits no fluorescence under UV light. These results tell us that no CDs or carbon nanosheets will be produced in this hydrothermal synthesis without the participation of ethanol (Note: complete depolymerization of PET to its monomers TPA and EG occurs during acidolysis if extra chemicals such as NaOH/HCl are sequentially introduced to remove continuously the produced monomers from the mixture [36,37], which is not the case here). Similarly, alcoholysis alone (i.e., PET in ethanol) cannot fully degrade PET under our synthesis conditions, given the complete alcoholysis of PET (using methanol in most cases) occurs only under the help of catalysts [38], supercritical fluids conditions (250–300 °C and 6–8 MPa) [39], and/or special solvent/agents (e.g., DMSO, NaOH) [40]. We also confirmed that without the presence of PET, ethanol and nitric acid alone would not produce CDs or other solid carbon materials either, which is consistent with some other reported work [41]. Instead, ethanol is converted to aldehyde, and then carboxylic acid via hydride abstraction and releases hydrogen (Caution: if not well diluted with water, this could lead to a vigorous reaction and become explosive). These observations suggest that ethanol helps promote the alcoholysis of PET polymer chains into fragments, while preventing the re-polymerization of PET fragments into large fragments due to the single hydroxyl group on its molecular structure, unlike ethylene glycol. As not all PET fragments are exhausted into oligomers or monomers, they are transformed into carbon nanosheets eventually during the next carbonization processes catalyzed by excessive nitric acid. As for the monomers after deep depolymerization, NO2 groups are further grafted onto their aromatic rings to produce less soluble 3-nitrophthalic acid or 3-nitrophthalates [36], which introduces nitrogen atoms in the later carbon framework (see Fig. 2b & c). Similar nitration of TPA or more complicated aromatics has been reported [37,42]. The hydrogen supplied by the reaction between ethanol (or ethylene glycol) and nitric acid allows hydrogen transfer to these –NO2 groups for their further reduction to –NH2 [37,43,44] Such transfer hydrogenation of aromatic nitro compounds in protic solvents like alcohols proceeds through hydroxylamine-like intermediates [45]. The nitration of aromatic rings in our carbon products was confirmed with the 1H NMR spectrum with nitrogen-associated proton peaks appearing near 8.4 and 9.6 ppm, together with peaks of aryl protons on carbon atoms near the nitrogen group between 7.1 and 8.2 ppm (Supp Fig. S6).

As addressed above, both nitric acid and ethanol are essential for successful transformation of PET to this PBSCC2H5OH structure. As shown in Supp Figs. S7a and S7b, when the quantity of ethanol or nitric acid is reduced from its original protocol (2 mL), the yield of CDs drops significantly, reflected by the decreased fluorescence signal from the received samples. The concentration of nitric acid seems to have a larger impact than ethanol on CD production. Nonetheless, no CDs will be produced when nitric acid or ethanol is completely absent even if the other compound stays at the same concentration as in the original protocol. The 8-h reaction time is also necessary to ensure the high yield of CDs, like in many other CD synthesis processes (Supp Fig. S7c). This synthesis condition is also required if targeting the overall degradation of PET and/or the received solid products. As shown in Supp Figs. S8a and 1- or 2-h reaction barely cause much degradation of PET. Even after a 4-h transformation, more than 30 % PET was left partially degraded, and the yield of CDs was very limited when compared to the PBSCHOC2H4OH sample after 8-h synthesis (Note: the presence of CDs causes an obvious mass loss near 200 °C in TGA as shown in Supp Fig. S8b). These TGA results (Supp Fig. S8a) also match the observations on the low fluorescence signal of CDs received with a shorter reaction time (Supp Fig. S7c). Therefore, both the optimal composition of nitric acid and ethanol and the synthesis conditions reported in our protocol are critical to guarantee the successful transformation of PET to the unique ball-sheet carbon network of PBSCC2H5OH.

Through hydrothermal synthesis, PET plastic waste was successfully transformed into CDs and conductive carbon nanosheets simultaneously with the former well intercalated in the latter as a connected ball-sheet carbon network. With the help of nitric acid and ethanol, the depolymerized PET oligomers/monomers are condensed into CDs packed with benzene rings in the core while grafted functional groups of –OH, –COOH, –NH, and O=C–NH < groups on the surface. The received unique carbon structure exhibits excellent electrical conductivity and impressive capacity to host ions after being assembled as supercapacitor electrodes. A specific capacity of 237 F/g and 198 F/g was achieved with these PBSC electrodes at the charge rate of 1 A/g and 2 A/g, respectively, which drops slowly under even higher rates. Excellent long-term cycling stability was also found with a 98 % capacity retention percentage after 12,000 cycles. With these accomplishments, our method provides new revenue to utilize and upcycle the discarded drinking bottles to help boost the collection and recycling of plastic waste to create a close-loop clean energy utilization system.

Yixian Pei: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yixin Liao: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jinge Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Haixu Zhong: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Data curation. Yu Yang: Validation, Data curation. Xiufang Wen: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Weimin Du: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shengnian Wang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation [OIA 2217824, 2418390], the Louisiana Board of Regents, ITRS [LEQSF(2023-26)-RD-B-02], and Louisiana Harrelson Family Professorship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
The following PDF file is available free of charge: supplementary table and figures on the fluorescence spectra of CDs, BET, XPS, FTIR, NMR, TGA results, and galvanostatic charge/discharge performance of PBSCs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2024.235252.
